title: The Lamplighter of Reykjavík
summary: A literary portrait of one of the last municipal lamplighters in Iceland, whose obsolete craft survives as a tourist curiosity, and who is quietly unsure whether being preserved is a form of honour or of taxidermy.
body:
Every evening from October to April, Þórunn walks the same four hundred metres of Reykjavík's old harbour quarter, carrying a brass pole that her grandfather carried before her, and she lights, by hand, the eleven gas lamps that the city has declined to convert to electricity. There is no practical reason for this. The lamps could be wired and forgotten in an afternoon. The reason they are not is that, two decades ago, a heritage committee decided the ritual was worth keeping, and so Þórunn's job exists in the curious space between employment and exhibit.

She is, on paper, a municipal contractor. In practice she is something closer to a living diorama, and she knows it. Tourists photograph her. A cruise company includes her round on its winter itinerary, billing it, to her mild irritation, as 'an authentic encounter with old Iceland'. She finds the adjective amusing. There is nothing authentic about being scheduled. Her grandfather lit lamps because the city was dark and people needed to see; she lights them because the city has decided that a certain kind of darkness is charming.

And yet she would be lying if she claimed to resent it. The truth is more tangled. On the coldest nights, when the harbour wind comes off the water like a reproach and the tourists have sensibly stayed indoors, she walks the route alone, and the work briefly becomes what it once was: not a performance but a small, necessary act against the dark. She lights the first lamp and the pool of yellow light it throws onto the wet cobbles is, for a moment, indistinguishable from the light her grandfather made. The century between them collapses. Then a lamp flickers, she adjusts the mantle, and the spell breaks; she remembers that the gas is piped, metered, and paid for by a committee.

What troubles her, on the nights she lets herself think about it, is not that the craft is dying. Crafts die; that is what crafts do, and sentimentality about it would be a kind of dishonesty. What troubles her is the suspicion that preservation is itself a quieter form of death. A craft that is kept alive deliberately, for its own sake, has already stopped being a craft and become a museum piece that happens to move. The lamps are no longer infrastructure. They are scenery. And she is no longer a worker so much as a curator of her own grandfather's labour, paid to reenact a usefulness that has been retired.

There is a particular tourist she remembers, an elderly man from Osaka who watched her light all eleven lamps without taking a single photograph. At the end he bowed, slightly, and said in careful English that in his city there had once been a man who repaired the wooden geta sandals that nobody wore any more, and that the man had been declared a Living National Treasure, and had hated the title. 'It made him a thing,' the man said. 'Before, he was a person who mended sandals. After, he was a sandal-mending that the country owned.' Then he wished her good evening and walked off into the dark she had just pushed back.

Þórunn has thought about that man often. She is not sentimental about her pole, which is heavy and cold and gives her a familiar ache between the shoulder blades by the eighth lamp. She does not believe she is preserving anything that matters to anyone but tourists and a committee. But on the alone nights, lighting the dark for no audience, she allows herself a heresy she would never say aloud to the cruise company: that the work is realest precisely when nobody is watching it being performed, and that an authenticity which requires a witness was never authenticity at all.

annotations.phrase: lights, by hand, the eleven gas lamps
annotations.meaningVi: thắp, bằng tay, mười một ngọn đèn gas (nghề thắp đèn đường cũ)
nhà thầu của chính quyền thành phố
đã từ chối chuyển đổi
ủy ban di sản
giữa việc làm và vật trưng bày — khoảng không gian mơ hồ định nghĩa công việc của cô
mô hình trưng bày sống — ẩn dụ tự châm biếm rằng cô như một mẫu vật bảo tàng biết cử động
lịch trình (chuyến đi)
sự khó chịu nhẹ
đích thực, nguyên bản

annotations.phrase: There is nothing authentic about being scheduled
annotations.meaningVi: Chẳng có gì đích thực ở việc được lên lịch — câu mỉa mai trung tâm của bài

annotations.phrase: a certain kind of darkness is charming
annotations.meaningVi: một loại bóng tối nào đó thật quyến rũ — thành phố giữ đèn vì thẩm mỹ chứ không vì nhu cầu
oán giận, bực bội về
rối rắm, phức tạp
như một lời trách móc — gió lạnh được nhân hóa
đá lát đường (loại tròn)
Một thế kỷ giữa hai người sụp đổ — khoảnh khắc quá khứ và hiện tại hòa làm một
lưới đèn măng-sông (bộ phận phát sáng của đèn gas)
phép màu tan vỡ — khoảnh khắc ảo tưởng kết thúc
được đo bằng đồng hồ (đo lượng tiêu thụ)
sự ủy mị, đa cảm thái quá
một dạng cái chết âm thầm hơn — luận điểm rằng việc bảo tồn cũng là một kiểu giết chết
cơ sở hạ tầng
phông cảnh, cảnh trí (trang trí)
người phụ trách/bảo quản (bảo tàng)
tái hiện, diễn lại
guốc gỗ geta (giày truyền thống Nhật Bản)
Báu vật Nhân văn Sống (danh hiệu Nhật trao cho nghệ nhân bậc thầy)
Nó biến ông thành một đồ vật — danh hiệu tước đi tư cách con người của nghệ nhân
điều dị giáo, quan điểm trái ngược với số đông

annotations.phrase: an authenticity which requires a witness
annotations.meaningVi: một sự đích thực cần có người chứng kiến — nghịch lý kết bài: cái gì cần khán giả thì không còn đích thực
cơn nhức giữa hai bả vai — chi tiết thể chất nhấn mạnh lao động thật
đẩy lùi (bóng tối)

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences with 'What...' for emphasis
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft) dùng 'What...' đưa thông tin quan trọng ra cuối câu để nhấn mạnh và tạo nhịp điệu phân tích, đặc trưng văn xuôi C1. Trong bài, Þórunn dùng cấu trúc này hai lần liên tiếp để đặt tương phản sắc bén.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A 'what'-cleft front-loads a placeholder and saves the real point for the end: 'What troubles her ... is not that the craft is dying ... What troubles her is the suspicion that preservation is itself a quieter form of death.' The repetition of the cleft frame across two sentences builds a deliberate, essayistic contrast that flat statements ('She is troubled by...') could not achieve.

translationVi:
Mỗi tối từ tháng Mười đến tháng Tư, Þórunn đi bộ trên đúng bốn trăm mét của khu cảng cũ Reykjavík, mang theo cây sào đồng mà ông cô từng mang trước cô, và cô thắp, bằng tay, mười một ngọn đèn gas mà thành phố đã từ chối chuyển sang điện. Chẳng có lý do thực tế nào cho việc này. Những ngọn đèn có thể được nối dây và quên đi trong một buổi chiều. Lý do chúng không được làm vậy là vì, hai thập kỷ trước, một ủy ban di sản đã quyết định rằng nghi thức này đáng được giữ lại, và thế là công việc của Þórunn tồn tại trong khoảng không gian kỳ lạ giữa việc làm và vật trưng bày.

Trên giấy tờ, cô là một nhà thầu của thành phố. Trong thực tế cô gần với một mô hình trưng bày sống hơn, và cô biết điều đó. Du khách chụp ảnh cô. Một hãng du thuyền đưa vòng đi của cô vào lịch trình mùa đông, quảng cáo nó, khiến cô hơi khó chịu, là 'một cuộc gặp gỡ đích thực với Iceland xưa'. Cô thấy tính từ ấy buồn cười. Chẳng có gì đích thực ở việc được lên lịch cả. Ông cô thắp đèn vì thành phố tối và người ta cần nhìn thấy; cô thắp đèn vì thành phố đã quyết định rằng một loại bóng tối nào đó thật quyến rũ.

Vậy mà cô sẽ nói dối nếu khẳng định mình oán ghét việc đó. Sự thật rối rắm hơn. Vào những đêm lạnh nhất, khi gió cảng thổi từ mặt nước như một lời trách móc và du khách đã khôn ngoan ở trong nhà, cô đi suốt lộ trình một mình, và công việc trong chốc lát trở lại như nó từng là: không phải một màn trình diễn mà là một hành động nhỏ, cần thiết chống lại bóng tối. Cô thắp ngọn đèn đầu tiên và vũng ánh sáng vàng nó hắt lên những viên đá lát ướt, trong một khoảnh khắc, không thể phân biệt với ánh sáng ông cô từng tạo ra. Một thế kỷ giữa hai người sụp đổ. Rồi một ngọn đèn chập chờn, cô chỉnh lại lưới đèn, và phép màu tan vỡ; cô nhớ ra rằng khí gas được dẫn ống, đo bằng đồng hồ, và được một ủy ban chi trả.

Điều làm cô bận lòng, vào những đêm cô cho phép mình nghĩ về nó, không phải là nghề đang chết. Các nghề thủ công đều chết; đó là điều các nghề thủ công làm, và ủy mị về điều đó sẽ là một dạng giả dối. Điều làm cô bận lòng là mối nghi ngờ rằng chính sự bảo tồn là một dạng cái chết âm thầm hơn. Một nghề được giữ sống một cách có chủ đích, vì chính nó, đã ngừng là một nghề và trở thành một hiện vật bảo tàng tình cờ biết cử động. Những ngọn đèn không còn là cơ sở hạ tầng. Chúng là phông cảnh. Và cô không còn là một người lao động mà là người bảo quản chính sức lao động của ông cô, được trả tiền để tái hiện một sự hữu dụng đã được cho về hưu.

Có một du khách cụ thể mà cô nhớ, một ông già đến từ Osaka đã xem cô thắp cả mười một ngọn đèn mà không chụp một tấm ảnh nào. Cuối cùng ông cúi đầu, khẽ, và nói bằng tiếng Anh cẩn thận rằng ở thành phố ông từng có một người sửa những đôi guốc gỗ geta mà chẳng còn ai đi nữa, và người ấy đã được phong là Báu vật Nhân văn Sống, và đã ghét danh hiệu đó. 'Nó biến ông thành một đồ vật,' người đàn ông nói. 'Trước đó, ông là một người sửa guốc. Sau đó, ông là một việc-sửa-guốc mà đất nước sở hữu.' Rồi ông chúc cô buổi tối tốt lành và bước đi vào bóng tối mà cô vừa mới đẩy lùi.

Þórunn đã nghĩ về người đàn ông ấy nhiều lần. Cô không ủy mị về cây sào của mình, vốn nặng và lạnh và gây cho cô một cơn nhức quen thuộc giữa hai bả vai khi đến ngọn đèn thứ tám. Cô không tin mình đang bảo tồn bất cứ thứ gì quan trọng với ai ngoài du khách và một ủy ban. Nhưng vào những đêm một mình, thắp sáng bóng tối cho không một khán giả nào, cô cho phép mình một điều dị giáo mà cô sẽ không bao giờ nói thành lời với hãng du thuyền: rằng công việc thật nhất chính vào lúc không ai nhìn nó được trình diễn, và rằng một sự đích thực cần có người chứng kiến thì chưa bao giờ là đích thực cả.

questions.prompt: Why does Þórunn's job of lighting the gas lamps still exist?
options: The harbour quarter has no electrical supply to power modern lamps
A heritage committee decided two decades ago that the ritual was worth keeping
The cruise companies pay the city directly to maintain the lamps
Her grandfather's contract legally requires the role to be passed down
questions.explanation: 'two decades ago, a heritage committee decided the ritual was worth keeping, and so Þórunn's job exists in the curious space between employment and exhibit.'

questions.prompt: What does Þórunn find amusing about the cruise company's description of her work as 'an authentic encounter with old Iceland'?
options: The company has never actually sent any tourists to watch her
Iceland was never as dark as the marketing implies
Something that is scheduled for tourists cannot, in her view, be authentic
The tourists are mostly from countries with their own lamplighting traditions
questions.explanation: 'She finds the adjective amusing. There is nothing authentic about being scheduled.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 2 contains her reaction to the word 'authentic'.

questions.prompt: What is the difference the passage draws between why her grandfather lit lamps and why Þórunn does?
options: He was paid more generously than she is by the modern committee
He lit them out of necessity for visibility; she lights them because the city finds a kind of darkness charming
He worked only in summer, whereas she works through the winter months
He used electricity, whereas she has reverted to gas for the tourists
questions.explanation: 'Her grandfather lit lamps because the city was dark and people needed to see; she lights them because the city has decided that a certain kind of darkness is charming.'
questions.hint: The final two sentences of paragraph 2 contrast the two motives.

questions.prompt: What happens on the coldest nights when the tourists have stayed indoors?
options: She abandons the route because the wind makes it too dangerous
The work briefly becomes a genuine act against the dark rather than a performance
She lights only the lamps nearest the cruise terminal
She is relieved of duty by a substitute contractor
questions.explanation: 'the work briefly becomes what it once was: not a performance but a small, necessary act against the dark.'

questions.prompt: What does the phrase 'The century between them collapses' refer to?
options: The physical decay of the hundred-year-old lamps
A moment when her light seems indistinguishable from her grandfather's, dissolving the time between them
The committee's decision to demolish the old harbour structures
A loss of memory in which she forgets which lamps she has lit
questions.explanation: 'the pool of yellow light it throws onto the wet cobbles is, for a moment, indistinguishable from the light her grandfather made. The century between them collapses.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 3, the moment with the first lamp.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what troubles Þórunn most?
options: That the craft is dying out
That she earns less than her grandfather did
That preservation itself may be a quieter form of death
That tourists photograph her without permission
questions.explanation: 'What troubles her ... is not that the craft is dying ... What troubles her is the suspicion that preservation is itself a quieter form of death.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 4 distinguishes two possible worries and names the real one.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean by saying the lamps are 'no longer infrastructure ... They are scenery'?
options: The lamps have been physically relocated to a stage set
The lamps no longer serve a functional purpose and exist only for their appearance
The lamps are now powered by decorative rather than functional gas
The lamps are maintained by set designers rather than engineers
questions.explanation: The contrast between 'infrastructure' (functional) and 'scenery' (decorative) captures that the lamps no longer do useful work — they only look the part.
questions.hint: Paragraph 4 uses both words to make a single point about function versus appearance.

questions.prompt: Why did the man from Osaka say the sandal-mender hated being named a Living National Treasure?
options: The title came with taxes he could not afford
The title turned him from a person into 'a thing' the country owned
The title forced him to train apprentices he did not want
The title required him to move to the capital
questions.explanation: 'It made him a thing ... Before, he was a person who mended sandals. After, he was a sandal-mending that the country owned.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 5 quotes the man's exact explanation.

questions.prompt: Notably, the man from Osaka 'watched her light all eleven lamps without taking a single photograph.' What does this detail emphasise?
options: That he was too elderly to operate a camera
That he, unlike other tourists, witnessed the act rather than consuming it as a spectacle
That photography was forbidden in the harbour quarter
That he intended to report her to the heritage committee
questions.explanation: His refusal to photograph sets him apart from the tourists who 'photograph her', marking him as someone who understood the act rather than collecting an image of it.
questions.hint: Contrast his behaviour with the tourists described in paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: What is the 'heresy' Þórunn allows herself on the nights she works alone?
options: That she would prefer the lamps to be converted to electricity
That the work is most real when nobody is watching it performed
That she resents her grandfather for leaving her the pole
That the heritage committee should be disbanded
questions.explanation: 'the work is realest precisely when nobody is watching it being performed, and that an authenticity which requires a witness was never authenticity at all.'
questions.hint: The final sentence states the heresy.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the writer NOT claiming about Þórunn's situation?
options: That her feelings about the work are genuinely conflicted
That the physical labour is real and tiring
That she straightforwardly resents being a tourist attraction
That moments of authentic meaning still occur for her
questions.explanation: The text explicitly resists this: 'she would be lying if she claimed to resent it. The truth is more tangled.' Her attitude is ambivalent, not simple resentment.
questions.hint: Look at the opening of paragraph 3, which qualifies any simple reading.

questions.prompt: The word 'reproach' in 'the harbour wind comes off the water like a reproach' most nearly means:
options: an expression of blame or disapproval
questions.explanation: A reproach is a rebuke or expression of disapproval; the simile personifies the cold wind as if it were scolding her.
questions.hint: Paragraph 3; think about how the wind is being personified.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the tone of the passage overall?
options: Nostalgic and uncritically celebratory of vanished crafts
Reflective and ambivalent, holding admiration and unease in tension
Angry and accusatory toward the heritage committee
Detached and purely factual, with no emotional content
questions.explanation: The passage neither celebrates nor condemns; it dwells in tangled ambivalence ('The truth is more tangled') about whether preservation honours or embalms.
questions.hint: Consider how the writer refuses both pure resentment and pure sentimentality.

questions.prompt: Which of these two paraphrases is closer to the writer's stance on preservation?
options: Preserving a dying craft is always wrong because it is dishonest
Deliberately preserving a craft can hollow out the very thing it means to save, even if some real meaning survives
Crafts should be allowed to die without any intervention whatsoever
Tourists are entirely responsible for ruining authentic traditions
questions.explanation: The writer suggests preservation risks turning a craft into 'a museum piece that happens to move', yet also grants that genuine meaning survives on the alone nights — a both/and position, not a blanket condemnation.
questions.hint: Balance paragraph 4's worry against paragraph 3's and the ending's concession.

questions.prompt: What does Þórunn's familiar 'ache between the shoulder blades by the eighth lamp' contribute to the passage?
options: It proves she is too old to continue the work
It grounds the abstract debate in real physical labour, complicating the idea that the work is mere performance
It explains why she resents the heritage committee
It shows the lamps are too heavy to be safe
questions.explanation: The bodily ache is concrete evidence that the labour is real, not pantomime — quietly arguing against the 'mere scenery' reading even as she entertains it.
questions.hint: Paragraph 6; consider what physical detail does to an argument about authenticity.

questions.prompt: The word 'sentimentality' (paragraph 4) is used by the writer to suggest that:
options: Þórunn is deeply emotional about the lamps
Mourning the death of crafts excessively would itself be a kind of dishonesty
The committee was motivated purely by feeling
Tourists are too sentimental about old Iceland
questions.explanation: 'sentimentality about it would be a kind of dishonesty' — she refuses to overstate the tragedy of a craft dying, since crafts naturally die.
questions.hint: Paragraph 4, where she discusses the death of crafts.

cloze.template:
Every evening from October to April, Þórunn walks the same four hundred metres of Reykjavík's old harbour quarter, carrying a brass pole that her grandfather carried before her, and she lights, by hand, the eleven gas lamps that the city has {{b1}} to convert to electricity. There is no practical reason for this. The lamps could be wired and forgotten in an afternoon. The reason they are not is that, two decades ago, a {{b2}} committee decided the ritual was worth keeping, and so Þórunn's job exists in the curious space between employment and exhibit.

She is, on paper, a municipal contractor. In practice she is something closer to a living {{b3}}, and she knows it. Tourists photograph her. A cruise company includes her round on its winter {{b4}}, billing it, to her mild irritation, as 'an authentic encounter with old Iceland'. She finds the adjective amusing. There is nothing {{b5}} about being scheduled. Her grandfather lit lamps because the city was dark and people needed to see; she lights them because the city has decided that a certain kind of darkness is charming.

And yet she would be lying if she claimed to {{b6}} it. The truth is more tangled. On the coldest nights, when the harbour wind comes off the water like a {{b7}} and the tourists have sensibly stayed indoors, she walks the route alone, and the work briefly becomes what it once was: not a performance but a small, necessary act against the dark. She lights the first lamp and the pool of yellow light it throws onto the wet cobbles is, for a moment, indistinguishable from the light her grandfather made. The century between them {{b8}}. Then a lamp flickers, she adjusts the mantle, and the spell breaks; she remembers that the gas is piped, {{b9}}, and paid for by a committee.

What troubles her, on the nights she lets herself think about it, is not that the craft is dying. Crafts die; that is what crafts do, and {{b10}} about it would be a kind of dishonesty. What troubles her is the suspicion that preservation is itself a quieter form of death. A craft that is kept alive deliberately, for its own sake, has already stopped being a craft and become a museum piece that happens to move. The lamps are no longer {{b11}}. They are scenery. And she is no longer a worker so much as a {{b12}} of her own grandfather's labour, paid to reenact a usefulness that has been retired.

There is a particular tourist she remembers, an elderly man from Osaka who watched her light all eleven lamps without taking a single photograph. At the end he bowed, slightly, and said in careful English that in his city there had once been a man who repaired the wooden geta sandals that nobody wore any more, and that the man had been declared a Living National {{b13}}, and had hated the title. 'It made him a thing,' the man said. 'Before, he was a person who mended sandals. After, he was a sandal-mending that the country owned.' Then he wished her good evening and walked off into the dark she had just pushed back.

Þórunn has thought about that man often. She is not sentimental about her pole, which is heavy and cold and gives her a familiar ache between the shoulder blades by the eighth lamp. She does not believe she is preserving anything that matters to anyone but tourists and a committee. But on the alone nights, lighting the dark for no audience, she allows herself a {{b14}} she would never say aloud to the cruise company: that the work is realest precisely when nobody is watching it being performed, and that an authenticity which requires a {{b15}} was never authenticity at all.

blanks.explanation: 'the city has declined to convert' — 'declined' is the measured, formal verb for an institution choosing not to act; 'refused' is too combative, 'neglected' implies oversight, 'hesitated' lacks finality.
'a heritage committee decided the ritual was worth keeping' — the body concerned with preserving the past.
'something closer to a living diorama' — a diorama is a staged scene; the adjective 'living' creates the self-mocking image of a moving display.
'on its winter itinerary' — the planned route of a tour; the fixed collocation for a cruise programme.
'There is nothing authentic about being scheduled' — echoes the cruise company's word 'authentic' to make the irony land.
'she would be lying if she claimed to resent it' — to feel bitter ill-will toward; the rest of the paragraph qualifies this resentment.
'comes off the water like a reproach' — personifies the wind as a rebuke; the chosen word carries the sense of moral disapproval the simile needs.
'The century between them collapses' — folds in on itself so past and present momentarily coincide.
'the gas is piped, metered, and paid for' — 'metered' specifically means measured by a meter, the precise utilities term.
'sentimentality about it would be a kind of dishonesty' — excessive, indulgent emotion; the word carries the critical edge the sentence needs.
'The lamps are no longer infrastructure. They are scenery.' — infrastructure (functional public works) is the precise contrast with decorative scenery.
'a curator of her own grandfather's labour' — a curator looks after museum pieces, extending the 'museum piece' image exactly.
'a Living National Treasure' — the actual title Japan confers on master artisans.
'she allows herself a heresy' — a belief contrary to accepted doctrine; sharper than 'doubt' or 'secret' and matches the rebellious tone.
'an authenticity which requires a witness' — 'witness' best fits 'requires a', carrying the legal/moral weight of someone attesting to truth.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Þórunn suspects that deliberately preserving a craft can turn it into 'a museum piece that happens to move', hollowing out the very thing preservation meant to save. Think of a tradition, skill, or place in your own community that is being kept alive mainly for visitors or for the record. Is that preservation keeping it genuinely alive, or quietly embalming it — and is there a way to tell the difference?
